sections.title: 1 · Vertebral Column & Thorax Bones
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

Questions cluster on: which features are UNIVERSAL to vertebrae vs. region-specific (cervical/thoracic/lumbar/sacral); the special features of the atlas (C1) and axis (C2); and the counts/parts of the thoracic cage.

The examiner loves regional "signatures": transverse foramen (all cervical), costal facets (thoracic), and the auricular surface (sacrum).

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Transverse foramen is present in EVERY cervical vertebra (C1–C7) — carries the vertebral artery (except C7, vein only)2020s Q4/28, 2021A Q3, 2021B Q41, sep2023 Q33 — recurs ~5×
Thoracic vertebrae have costal facets / demifacets for the ribs (their signature feature)2021A Q3, 2020s Q28, 2022 Q29, sep2023 Q33
Vertebra prominens = C7 (longest, non-bifid spinous process)2020 Q8
Carotid tubercle (Chassaignac) = transverse process of C62020 Q10, 2021A Q17
Fovea for the dens (facet for odontoid) is on the anterior arch of the ATLAS (C1)2021B Q18, 2023 Q30
Atlas (C1) has NO body and NO spinous process; anterior + posterior arch, lateral masses2020 Q38, 2022 Q36
The dens (odontoid process) belongs to the AXIS (C2) — it is NOT a common feature of vertebrae2020 Q20, 2023 Q2
Auricular (ear-shaped) surface = lateral sacrum (and ilium) → sacroiliac joint2020 Q6/36, 2023 Q35
Sacrum: promontory, sacral canal, 4 pairs of sacral foramina, auricular surface (NOT "iliac tuberosity")2020 Q36, sep2023 Q33
Thoracic cage = 12 pairs of ribs (24 total); 7 true, 3 false, 2 floating; sternum = manubrium + body + xiphoid2020 Q30, 2022 Q38
Vertebral artery travels in the transverse foramina (foramina transversaria)2020 Q10, sep2023 Q7
Accessory process of lumbar vertebrae lies posterior to the transverse (costal) process2022 Q34

REGIONAL "SIGNATURES" — THE SINGLE FEATURE THE EXAM USES TO ID EACH REGION

RegionDistinguishing feature
CervicalTransverse foramen (all 7); bifid spinous process (C3–C6); C1 no body, C2 has dens; C7 vertebra prominens
ThoracicCostal facets/demifacets on body + transverse processes; heart-shaped body; long downward spinous process
LumbarMassive kidney-shaped body; no transverse foramen, no costal facet; mamillary + accessory processes
SacrumFused; promontory, sacral canal, 4 pairs anterior/posterior foramina, auricular surface for SI joint
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False — dens belongs only to C2 (axis).
False — lumbar vertebrae have no transverse foramen.
False — hip bone = ilium + ischium + pubis; sacrum only articulates with it.
False — that ridge is on the ilium; sacrum has the auricular surface.

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • Build a 4-row table (cervical/thoracic/lumbar/sacral) and force yourself to write each region's single signature — that is how every "which vertebra has X" question is set.
  • Memorise atlas vs axis as a contrast pair: C1 = no body/no spine, has fovea for dens; C2 = has the dens.
  • Lock in the thorax numbers (12 pairs, 24 ribs, 7-3-2, sternum = 3 parts) — pure recall marks.
Mnemonics
Rib groups: "7 true, 3 false, 2 free" (7 vertebrosternal, 3 vertebrochondral, 2 floating).
Sacrum surface: Sacrum = "ear" bone → AUricular surface (think "AU" = your ear listening).

sections.title: 2 · Bones of the Upper Limb
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

Landmark localisation is everything here: "which structure belongs to / does NOT belong to" the clavicle, scapula, humerus, radius, ulna, carpus. The examiner repeatedly swaps a landmark onto the wrong bone (e.g. "spiral groove on the ulna", "greater trochanter on the humerus").

The carpal bones (proximal vs distal row) and the carpal tunnel walls also recur.

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Spiral (radial) groove is on the HUMERUS (posterior shaft) — never the ulna2021B Q1, 2020s Q5, sep2020 Q23
Olecranon belongs to the ULNA, not the humerus (humerus has the olecranon FOSSA)2020 Q49
"Greater trochanter" and "surgical neck" are femur/lower-limb terms — never on the humerus2021A Q45, 2021B Q15
Distal radius carries: ulnar notch, styloid process, Lister's (dorsal) tubercle, groove for EPL tendon2020 Q4, 2021A Q18, 2021B Q44, 2022 Q37, sep2020 Q39
Radial tuberosity (biceps) is at the PROXIMAL end of the radius; radial head has fovea + circumference2020 Q22, 2021B Q44, sep2020 Q5
Groove for ulnar nerve lies BEHIND the medial epicondyle of the humerus2021B Q1/34, sep2020 Q23
Groove for radial nerve lies behind the LATERAL epicondyle2021B Q44, sep2020 Q23
Deltoid tuberosity is on the humeral SHAFT — not the distal end2021A Q45
Clavicle landmarks: conoid tubercle, trapezoid line, groove for subclavius, impression for costoclavicular lig. (NOT "lateral margin")2020 Q2, 2022 Q3/4, 2023 Q6
Scapula: subscapular fossa on the anterior (costal) surface; supra-/infraspinous fossae posterior; coracoid superior to glenoid2022 Q32, 2021B Q16, 2023 Q19, sep2023 Q16
Triceps long head origin = infraglenoid tubercle; biceps long head = supraglenoid tubercle2020 Q48, 2023 Q19
Proximal carpal row = Scaphoid, Lunate, Triquetrum, Pisiform. Hamate & Trapezium are DISTAL row2022 Q31, sep2020 Q40, sep2023 Q32
Carpal tunnel: trapezium + scaphoid (radial) & hamate + pisiform (ulnar), roofed by the flexor retinaculum2020 Q28, 2023 Q13
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False (humerus).
mixed — Lister's tubercle IS distal (dorsal); the radial tuberosity is PROXIMAL.
False — lower-limb terms planted on the humerus.
False — hamate is distal row.
False — it is superior/anterior to the glenoid.

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • Draw each long bone (humerus, radius, ulna) and label proximal vs distal landmarks — the exam almost always attacks the proximal/distal split.
  • Make a "wrong-bone" flashcard set: for every landmark, note which bone it is truly on, because the trap is always a landmark on the wrong bone.
  • Memorise carpal rows with the classic mnemonic and separately learn the four carpal-tunnel corner bones.
Mnemonics
Carpal bones (proximal→distal, lateral→medial): "Some Lovers Try Positions That They Cannot Handle" = Scaphoid, Lunate, Triquetrum, Pisiform / Trapezium, Trapezoid, Capitate, Hamate.
Nerve grooves: Ulnar nerve → medial epicondyle ("funny bone" is on the inside); Radial nerve → radial/spiral groove on the shaft.

sections.title: 3 · Bones of the Lower Limb
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

Same landmark-localisation game as the upper limb, plus one recurring biomechanics item: the angle of inclination and coxa valga/vara ↔ genu varum/valgum relationship.

The tibia-vs-fibula split is a guaranteed question, and the tarsal bones (which belongs to which row) recur.

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Fibular notch is on the distal TIBIA (for the inferior tibiofibular joint) — the fibula has a STYLOID PROCESS at its head, not a fibular notch2020 Q33, 2021A Q36, 2021B Q40, sep2020 Q40, sep2023 Q9
Proximal femur = head (with fovea), neck, greater + lesser trochanter, trochanteric fossa, intertrochanteric line/crest ("surgical neck" & "fovea of greater trochanter" are wrong/humeral terms)2020 Q5, 2021A Q24
Adductor tubercle = superior to the MEDIAL femoral condyle2021A Q1, 2021B Q2, sep2020 Q39
Trochanteric fossa lies on the medial surface of the greater trochanter2021B Q9
Angle of inclination: LARGE → coxa valga + genu varum; SMALL → coxa vara + genu valgum2021A Q5, 2021B Q10
Gluteal tuberosity = lateral lip of the linea aspera, below the greater trochanter (posterior femur)2022 Q33, 2021B Q2, 2023 Q1
Linea aspera is on the POSTERIOR surface of the femur2023 Q1
Tibia carries: intercondylar eminence, medial/lateral condyles, tibial tuberosity, articular surfaces, fibular notch (NOT a "styloid process")sep2023 Q9, 2021B Q40
Tarsus (7 bones): talus, calcaneus, navicular, cuboid, 3 cuneiforms. Pisiform is a CARPAL bone, not tarsalsep2020 Q7, 2020 Q41
Talus: no muscle attachments; tarsal sinus on inferior surface; malleolar facets laterally; groove for FHL posteriorly; Achilles inserts on the CALCANEUS (not talus)sep2023 Q25
Hip bone = ilium + ischium + pubis (three bones fused at the acetabulum); the sacrum is NOT part of it2020 Q46, sep2020 Q40
Posterior border of the hip bone: ischial spine, ischial tuberosity, greater/lesser sciatic notches (NOT the iliopubic eminence)sep2023 Q19
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False — it is on the distal tibia.
False — femur has the anatomical neck + fovea on the HEAD.
False — medial femoral condyle.
False — it inserts on the calcaneal tuberosity.
False.

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • Learn tibia vs fibula as a strict two-column contrast — the exam ALWAYS tests the notch/styloid swap.
  • Memorise the inclination rule as a single sentence and rehearse it both directions (valga↔varum, vara↔valgum).
  • For the femur, drill proximal landmarks (head/neck/2 trochanters/fossa) vs posterior shaft (linea aspera, gluteal tuberosity, pectineal line).
Mnemonics
Tarsal bones: "Tall Californians Never Take Shortcuts, Cross-country" → Talus, Calcaneus, Navicular, 3 Cuneiforms, Cuboid.
Inclination: Big angle = "valGa" opens the leg out at the hip but knees bow IN → genu varum. (Big hip angle → opposite at knee.)

sections.title: 4 · Skull — Dome (Calvaria) & Cranial Base (Internal)
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

This is a FORAMEN-CONTENTS exam. The single most repeated question type across all seven papers is "which nerve/artery passes through which foramen." Learn the foramina table until it is automatic.

Secondary items: which fossa (anterior/middle/posterior) a structure sits in, the cranial sutures, dural venous sinuses (sigmoid → jugular foramen), and arachnoid granulations.

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Jugular foramen = CN IX, X, XI + IJV (the most-repeated single foramen)2021A Q7/29/33, 2021B Q6, 2023 Q34, sep2020 Q9
Foramen ovale = V3 (mandibular n.); rotundum = V2; spinosum = middle meningeal a.2023 Q4/15/27, sep2023 Q30
Internal acoustic meatus = CN VII + CN VIII only (glossopharyngeal is NOT here — it is jugular)2021B Q6, 2022 Q26, sep2020 Q2
Superior orbital fissure transmits V1 but NOT V2 (V2 = rotundum)2023 Q39, sep2023 Q14
Cribriform plate = olfactory (CN I) filaments2022 Q28, sep2023 Q5
Sella turcica / hypophyseal fossa is part of the SPHENOID bone (middle fossa)sep2023 Q17/30
Sutures: coronal (frontal–parietal), sagittal (parietal–parietal), lambdoid (parietal–occipital), squamous (temporal–parietal)2021B Q29, 2023 Q34
Groove for the sigmoid sinus is in the posterior fossa; sigmoid sinus drains into the jugular foramen → IJV2021A Q23, sep2020 Q26, sep2023 Q17
Arachnoid (Pacchionian) granulations lie alongside the superior sagittal sinus groove2022 Q30
Foramen cecum is in the anterior cranial fossasep2023 Q1

THE FORAMINA TABLE — HIGHEST-YIELD CONTENT IN THE WHOLE EXAM

Foramen / openingTransmits
Cribriform plate (ethmoid)Olfactory nerve (CN I) filaments
Optic canalOptic nerve (CN II) + ophthalmic artery
Superior orbital fissureCN III, IV, V1 (ophthalmic), VI + ophthalmic veins (NOT V2)
Foramen rotundumMaxillary nerve (V2)
Foramen ovaleMandibular nerve (V3)
Foramen spinosumMiddle meningeal artery
Foramen lacerumInternal carotid a. passes ABOVE it; deep petrosal / sympathetic carotid plexus
Internal acoustic meatusCN VII (facial) + CN VIII (vestibulocochlear) + labyrinthine a.
Jugular foramenCN IX, X, XI + internal jugular vein + sigmoid/inferior petrosal sinuses
Hypoglossal canalCN XII (hypoglossal)
Foramen magnumMedulla/spinal cord, vertebral arteries, spinal roots of CN XI
Stylomastoid foramenFacial nerve (CN VII) exits

WHICH CRANIAL FOSSA HOLDS WHAT

Cranial fossaKey contents / openings
AnteriorFrontal lobes, cribriform plate, crista galli, foramen cecum, orbital plates
MiddleTemporal lobes, sella turcica/hypophyseal fossa (sphenoid), optic canal, SOF, rotundum, ovale, spinosum, lacerum
PosteriorCerebellum/brainstem, foramen magnum, internal acoustic meatus, jugular foramen, hypoglossal canal, groove for sigmoid sinus, clivus
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False — CN IX exits via the jugular foramen.
False — via foramen spinosum.
False — middle fossa.
False — V2 = foramen rotundum.
False — sphenoid.

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • Build the foramina table as your #1 Anki priority — cloze each foramen↔content pair. Expect 4–6 marks per paper from this alone.
  • Learn the three fossae as containers, then slot each foramen into its fossa (all of rotundum/ovale/spinosum/lacerum/SOF/optic = middle fossa).
  • Pair the four sutures with their two bones each; the exam gives them verbatim.
Mnemonics
Superior orbital fissure: "Live Free To Sit Nakedly" won't help — just remember SOF = CN 3, 4, V1, 6 (all the eye-movers + ophthalmic sensation).
Jugular foramen nerves: "9, 10, 11 go out the jugular door."
Cranial nerve exits (I→XII): "Cribriform, Optic canal, SOF, SOF, Rotundum, Ovale, IAM, IAM, Jugular, Jugular, Jugular, Hypoglossal."

sections.title: 5 · Skull Base (External), Orbit, Nasal Cavity & Facial Skeleton
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

Three recurring themes: (1) the bony palate and external base landmarks; (2) the orbit and the nasal meatus drainage (which sinus/duct opens where); and (3) the viscerocranium — which bones are "facial", plus the vomer and nasal septum. The pterygopalatine fossa and its communications also appear.

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Bony palate landmarks: incisive foramen, greater/lesser palatine foramina, posterior nasal spine. The UVULA is soft tissue, NOT a bony-palate landmark2023 Q8/22
Vomer forms the (postero-inferior) nasal septum; it is an unpaired facial bonesep2023 Q24/28
Styloid process belongs to the TEMPORAL bonesep2023 Q27
Viscerocranium (facial bones): maxilla, mandible, nasal, lacrimal, zygomatic, palatine, vomer, inferior nasal concha. Parietal & hyoid are NOT facial bones2020 Q25
Anterior nasal spine belongs to the maxilla; lingula belongs to the mandible2022 Q39
Inferior nasal concha does NOT help form the orbit2022 Q7
Nasolacrimal duct → inferior meatus; maxillary sinus → middle meatus; posterior ethmoidal cells → superior meatus2020 Q18, 2023 Q12/14, sep2020 Q8
Carotid canal (external base, temporal bone) transmits the internal carotid arterysep2023 Q2/30
Auricular surface (for the SC joint region) sits anterior to the mastoid process on the temporal bone; occipital condyles flank the foramen magnum2020 Q6
Clivus is formed by sphenoid + occipital bones (not temporal)sep2023 Q30

NASAL MEATUS DRAINAGE — A REPEATED FAVOURITE

Meatus / recessWhat drains into it
Superior meatusPosterior ethmoidal air cells
Middle meatusFrontal sinus, maxillary sinus, anterior + middle ethmoidal cells (hiatus semilunaris)
Inferior meatusNasolacrimal duct
Spheno-ethmoidal recessSphenoid sinus

ORBIT & PTERYGOPALATINE FOSSA

StructureKey facts
Orbital floorInfraorbital groove/canal; formed mainly by maxilla (+ zygomatic, palatine)
Bones forming the orbitFrontal, sphenoid, zygomatic, maxilla, lacrimal, ethmoid, palatine — NOT the inferior nasal concha
Pterygopalatine fossaMedial wall = palatine bone; communicates with orbit (inferior orbital fissure), nasal cavity (sphenopalatine foramen), infratemporal fossa (pterygomaxillary fissure), middle fossa (rotundum), oral cavity (palatine canals)
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False — it is a soft-palate structure.
False.
False.
False — frontal → middle meatus; sphenoid → spheno-ethmoidal recess.
False — sphenoid + occipital.

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • Memorise nasal drainage as one small table — it is nearly free marks and gets reused.
  • Learn the 8 pairs/unpaired facial bones as a fixed list, then the two classic distractors (parietal, hyoid) that are NOT facial.
  • Keep the pterygopalatine fossa's 5 communications on one card — the "which does it connect to" question recurs.
Mnemonics
Facial bones (14): "Virgil Can Not Make My Pet Zebra Laugh" → Vomer, Conchae(inf), Nasal, Maxilla, Mandible, Palatine, Zygomatic, Lacrimal (pairs where relevant).
Meatus drainage: Nasolacrimal duct is "Nasty Low" → inferior meatus. Most sinuses drain to the MIDDLE meatus.

sections.title: 6 · Joints of the Trunk & Shoulder Girdle
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

Trunk: the craniovertebral (atlanto-occipital / atlanto-axial) ligament complex is the single most-tested joint region — cruciform ligament, alar ligaments, apical ligament, tectorial membrane, and the suboccipital triangle contents. Also the spinal ligaments (ALL/PLL, ligamentum flavum, nuchal ligament) and how vertebrae articulate.

Shoulder girdle: glenohumeral reinforcing ligaments, the coracoclavicular complex (conoid + trapezoid), and the classification of the AC and SC joints.

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Cruciform (cruciate) ligament of the atlas = transverse ligament of atlas + superior & inferior longitudinal bands (NOT the posterior longitudinal ligament / atlanto-occipital ligaments)2020 Q43, sep2020 Q32, sep2023 Q10
Alar ligaments run from the apex/sides of the dens to the occipital condyles (limit rotation)2021B Q21, 2023 Q20/30
Vertebral artery + C1 (suboccipital) nerve pass through the suboccipital triangle2021A Q43, 2021B Q21/26, 2023 Q28
Posterior longitudinal ligament runs along the posterior surface of the vertebral BODIES (inside canal) — NOT between the arches2023 Q3/17/28
Ligamentum flavum connects the laminae (vertebral arches)2021B Q30, 2023 Q27
Nuchal ligament = superior continuation of the supraspinous ligament (an independent cervical ligament)2020 Q24, 2022 Q24
Intervertebral disc = outer anulus fibrosus (fibrocartilage rings) + inner nucleus pulposus2021A Q20
Vertebrae articulate by BOTH amphiarthroses (bodies, via discs) and diarthroses (facet joints)2022 Q25
Costovertebral joint of the rib head has a radiate ligament; costotransverse joints have their own ligamentssep2023 Q2
Glenohumeral joint is reinforced by the coracohumeral + gleno­humeral ligaments (the coracoacromial ligament is an "arch", NOT a GH reinforcement)2020 Q50, sep2020 Q14/41
Coracoclavicular ligament = conoid + trapezoid parts2021B Q12, sep2020 Q41
AC joint = plane synovial joint (diarthrosis); SC joint = saddle synovial joint with an articular disc2022 Q19
Glenoid labrum deepens the glenoid cavity; shoulder communicates with the subscapular bursasep2020 Q37
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False — between the bodies (ligamentum flavum is between arches).
False — it is the upward continuation of the PLL, from the body of the axis to the occipital bone.
False.
False — it forms the coracoacromial arch above it.
False — it is a synovial (plane) joint.

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • Draw the craniovertebral ligaments in layers from anterior→posterior (apical → cruciform → tectorial membrane) — this is where most trunk-joint marks live.
  • Contrast the three "between" ligaments: PLL between bodies, ligamentum flavum between laminae, nuchal/supraspinous between spinous tips.
  • For the shoulder girdle, separate GH-joint ligaments from AC/CC/SC ligaments so you don't mix them.
Mnemonics
Coracoclavicular: "Con-Trap" = Conoid + Trapezoid (medial cone, lateral trapezoid).
Ligamentum flavum: "Flavum = laminae" (both have an "l"). PLL = posterior of the bodies.

sections.title: 7 · Joints of the Upper & Lower Limbs
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

Elbow & radioulnar: available movements (elbow = flexion/extension only; pronation/supination happen at the radioulnar joints), the annular + quadrate ligaments, and the distal radioulnar joint's triangular fibrocartilage.

Hip: it is a ball-and-socket joint; the three capsular ligaments (iliofemoral strongest/anterior; ischiofemoral; pubofemoral), the transverse acetabular ligament, and the intracapsular ligament of the head of the femur (obturator artery).

Knee & ankle: cruciate + collateral ligaments, menisci + transverse ligament, suprapatellar bursa, and the talocrural joint's dorsiflexion/plantarflexion with the deltoid (medial) ligament.

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Elbow (humeroulnar + humeroradial) = hinge → flexion/extension ONLY. Pronation/supination occur at the radioulnar joints2021A Q48, 2022 Q17/18
Annular ligament holds the radial head against the radial notch of the ulna; the quadrate ligament connects the radial notch to the radius neck (proximal radioulnar joint)sep2020 Q19, 2022 Q23, 2023 Q33
Distal radioulnar joint = pronation/supination; contains the triangular fibrocartilage (articular disc)2022 Q17, 2023 Q20
Humeroradial joint involves the capitulum; humeroulnar joint involves the trochleasep2020 Q19
Hip joint = ball-and-socket (spheroidal) — NOT ellipsoid/hinge2020 Q37, 2023 Q21
Iliofemoral ligament is the strongest hip ligament and reinforces it anteriorly/superiorly (limits extension)sep2020 Q11/43, 2023 Q13, 2021A Q47
Transverse acetabular ligament completes the lunate (crescent) articular surface of the acetabulum2021A Q47, 2021B Q14
Ligament of the head of the femur is intracapsular and carries a branch of the obturator artery2021B Q13, 2023 Q12, sep2020 Q43
ACL attaches to the tibial (intercondylar) eminence and to the medial side of the LATERAL femoral condyle; prevents anterior tibial translationsep2020 Q3, 2023 Q38
The two menisci are joined anteriorly by the transverse ligament of the knee2023 Q38
Medial (tibial) collateral ligament resists VALGUS force at the kneesep2023 Q23
Suprapatellar bursa COMMUNICATES with the knee-joint cavity; patellar retinacula are quadriceps expansions that stabilise the patella2021A Q47, 2021B Q13, 2022 Q22, sep2020 Q3
Talocrural (ankle) joint = hinge → dorsiflexion/plantarflexion; medial (deltoid) ligament runs from the medial malleolus to talus/navicular/calcaneus2020 Q34, 2023 Q29, sep2023 Q3
Arcuate popliteal ligament IS a knee ligament (distractor "not in the knee")2020 Q19
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False — the ILIOFEMORAL (Y-ligament) is strongest.
False — they are capsular/extracapsular; only the ligament of the head of the femur is intracapsular.
False — that is the radioulnar joint.
False — it does.
False — the tibiofibular joint is separate; the knee is femur–tibia–patella.

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • For each joint learn a 3-line card: classification → movements → key ligaments. The exam asks all three.
  • Hip ligaments: fix "iliofemoral = strongest = anterior" first, then use it to reject every "ischiofemoral is strongest" trap.
  • Keep intracapsular vs extracapsular straight for both hip (LHF intracapsular) and knee (cruciates intracapsular but extrasynovial).
Mnemonics
Hip ligament strength: "I Am Strong" → Iliofemoral is strongest, Anterior.
Cruciates: ACL stops the tibia sliding Anteriorly; PCL stops it sliding Posteriorly.
Ankle medial ligament: Deltoid = medial (think "delta" = Greek D = "medial Down the inside").

sections.title: 8 · Muscles of the Head & Neck
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

Muscles of mastication (temporalis, masseter, medial/lateral pterygoid) with their trigeminal (V3) innervation and specific actions; muscles of facial expression (all CN VII, incl. buccinator and occipitofrontalis); the scalene group; sternocleidomastoid; and the supra-/infrahyoid muscles.

The examiner repeatedly probes innervation (V3 mastication vs VII facial expression) and specific actions (lateral pterygoid = lateral movement/protrusion/opening).

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Muscles of mastication (temporalis, masseter, medial & lateral pterygoid) are all innervated by the mandibular nerve (V3)2020 Q7, 2021A Q10, 2022 Q15, 2023 Q36
Masseter: elevates/closes the mandible; origin = zygomatic arch, insertion = lateral (outer) surface of the ramus/angle (NOT the pterygoid process — that is the pterygoids)2021A Q10, sep2023 Q20
Lateral pterygoid produces protrusion + side-to-side (lateral) movements and initiates opening2022 Q15/20
Temporalis: origin temporal fossa, insertion coronoid process, innervated by V3 (deep temporal) — NOT the facial nerve2023 Q36
Muscles of facial expression are all innervated by the facial nerve (CN VII), including buccinatorsep2023 Q26, 2023 Q26
Occipitofrontalis (epicranius) is the scalp/calvarial muscle; frontalis has no bony origin and blends into the epicranial aponeurosis2020 Q16, 2023 Q26
Platysma is a superficial muscle of the neck, innervated by the facial nerve (CN VII)2020 Q3, sep2023 Q12
Sternocleidomastoid: motor = accessory nerve (CN XI) + proprioception from cervical plexus; inserts on the mastoid process; one side turns the head to the OPPOSITE side2021A Q32, sep2023 Q29
Anterior scalene: origin = transverse processes C3–C6; insertion = 1st rib between the grooves for the subclavian artery and vein; innervated by cervical/brachial plexus (accessory respiratory muscle)2020 Q21, 2021A Q11/14/16, sep2020 Q27
Scalene triangle (between anterior & middle scalene) transmits the brachial plexus + subclavian artery2021A Q28
Suprahyoids: geniohyoid, mylohyoid, stylohyoid (pulls hyoid up & back), digastric; infrahyoids: sternohyoid, omohyoid, thyrohyoid, sternothyroid2020 Q45, sep2023 Q29
Rectus capitis anterior assists flexion of the head2021A Q6, sep2020 Q10
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False — mastication = V3; facial expression = CN VII.
False — that is the medial/lateral pterygoid; masseter = zygomatic arch.
False — to the opposite side (and tilts to the same side).
False — cervical/brachial plexus (C4–C6).
False — it is a superficial neck muscle of facial-expression type (CN VII).

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • Split the head muscles by innervation first: V3 = the 4 chewers; CN VII = everything for expression + platysma. That one split answers most questions.
  • Learn each masticatory muscle's single "trick" action (lateral pterygoid = protrude/open/side-to-side).
  • Memorise anterior-scalene attachments word-for-word (C3–C6 → 1st rib between subclavian a. & v.) — it appears in almost every paper.
Mnemonics
Mastication: "M.T. cave" chews → Masseter, Temporalis, medial + lateral pterygoid; all V3.
Lateral pterygoid: The odd one out that OPENS the jaw (Lateral = Lowers/opens).
SCM turn: "Same side tilt, Opposite side turn."

sections.title: 9 · Muscles of the Back
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

Two groups dominate: (1) the superficial/intermediate back muscles that move the scapula or ribs — trapezius, latissimus dorsi, rhomboids, levator scapulae, serratus posterior superior/inferior — tested by their nerve and action; (2) the deep suboccipital muscles (rectus capitis posterior major/minor, obliquus capitis superior/inferior) with their suboccipital-nerve supply.

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Trapezius: innervated by the accessory nerve (CN XI) + C3–C4; retracts/rotates/elevates the scapula (pulls it toward the vertebral column)2021A Q15, sep2023 Q29
Latissimus dorsi: thoracodorsal nerve; inserts into the floor of the intertubercular groove; extends/adducts/medially rotates the arm2021A Q22/37, 2020s multiple
Rhomboid major & minor: dorsal scapular nerve; retract the scapula2021A Q27, sep2020 Q15
Levator scapulae: dorsal scapular nerve (+ C3–C4)2022 Q5
Serratus posterior SUPERIOR elevates the ribs (inspiration); serratus posterior INFERIOR depresses the ribs2021B Q5, 2023 Q3, sep2023 Q15
Suboccipital muscles (rectus capitis posterior major & minor, obliquus capitis superior & inferior) are all innervated by the suboccipital nerve (C1 dorsal ramus)2021A Q21/43, 2022 Q14, 2023 Q3, sep2023 Q15
Obliquus capitis superior/inferior + rectus capitis posterior major bound the suboccipital triangle (contains vertebral artery + C1 nerve)2021A Q43, 2021B Q21/26
Obliquus capitis inferior connects the spinous process of the axis (C2) to the transverse process of the atlas (C1) — the only "capitis" muscle not attaching to the skull2021A Q21
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False — it depresses them; the SUPERIOR one elevates.
False — dorsal scapular nerve (long thoracic → serratus anterior).
False — accessory nerve (CN XI).
False — suboccipital nerve (C1).

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • Tabulate the scapular movers by NERVE (accessory → trapezius; dorsal scapular → rhomboids + levator scapulae; long thoracic → serratus anterior; thoracodorsal → lat dorsi).
  • Memorise serratus posterior superior = inspiration (elevate), inferior = expiration (depress).
  • Learn the suboccipital triangle borders + contents together — it links back muscles to trunk joints and recurs in both chapters.
Mnemonics
Scapular nerves: "DR LAT" — Dorsal scapular = Rhomboids/Levator; thoracoDorsal = LATissimus.
Serratus posterior: Superior = Suck in (inspire, elevate); Inferior = Iforce out (depress).

sections.title: 10 · Muscles of the Pelvis & Hip (Gluteal Region + Iliopsoas + Adductors)
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

The gluteal muscles and their nerves/insertions; the iliopsoas; the adductor group and its dual innervation (adductor magnus); and the composition of the pes anserinus. Insertions (greater vs lesser trochanter) and nerve supply (superior vs inferior gluteal) are the recurring hooks.

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Iliopsoas (psoas major + iliacus) inserts on the lesser trochanter; iliacus = femoral nerve; it is the main hip flexor2020 Q23, 2021A Q34/40, 2021B Q23, sep2020 Q16, sep2023 Q13
Psoas major originates from T12–L5 (bodies/transverse processes/discs)2023 Q13
Gluteus medius & minimus insert on the greater trochanter; both innervated by the SUPERIOR gluteal nerve; they abduct the hip2021A Q25, 2021B Q20, sep2020 Q16
Gluteus maximus: INFERIOR gluteal nerve; extends the hip2021A Q19, sep2020 Q16
Piriformis is innervated by the sacral plexus (nerve to piriformis, S1–S2)2021A Q25, 2021B Q20
Adductor group (longus, brevis, magnus, gracilis, pectineus) = obturator nerve; adductor magnus has a DUAL supply (obturator + sciatic/tibial); pectineus also femoral2021B Q36, 2023 Q40, sep2020 Q30
Adductor magnus (hamstring part) originates from the ischial tuberositysep2020 Q30
Sartorius: origin = ASIS; femoral nerve; flexes hip & knee; contributes to the pes anserinus2021B Q8/17, 2023 Q31, sep2023 Q13
Pes anserinus = Sartorius + Gracilis + Semitendinosus (NOT iliacus, NOT semimembranosus)2020 Q27, 2021A Q31, 2021B Q4, sep2020 Q6
Sartorius and gracilis are the two muscles that are NOT adductors-only distractors: gracilis IS an adductor; sartorius is a flexor2023 Q40
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False — superior gluteal nerve (only gluteus maximus = inferior).
False — iliac fossa; the ASIS is the origin of SARTORIUS.
False — lesser trochanter.
False — it is a hip+knee flexor, femoral nerve.
False — SGT: sartorius, gracilis, semitendinosus.

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • Fix the two trochanter insertions: iliopsoas → lesser; gluteus medius/minimus (+ piriformis) → greater. Half these questions hinge on it.
  • Superior gluteal nerve = medius + minimus + tensor fasciae latae; inferior gluteal nerve = maximus only. Memorise as a pair.
  • Cement pes anserinus (SGT) and use it to instantly kill any "iliacus/semimembranosus" distractor.
Mnemonics
Pes anserinus: "Say Grace before Tea" → Sartorius, Gracilis, semiTendinosus.
Gluteal nerves: Superior gluteal → the two smaller glutei (Medius/Minimus + TFL); Inferior → the big one (Maximus).
Trochanters: "Psoas → Lesser" (both slim); "Glutei → Greater."

sections.title: 11 · Muscles of the Thorax & Abdomen
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

The diaphragm and its three openings (which structure passes at which level) is the flagship topic. Then the anterior abdominal wall: the rectus sheath and the arcuate line (of Douglas), the linea semilunaris, the cremaster, and the inguinal canal walls. Abdominal-muscle ACTIONS and the intercostals/serratus anterior also appear.

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Aortic hiatus (T12) lies BETWEEN the two crura and transmits the aorta (+ thoracic duct, azygos); it is NOT the esophageal hiatus2021B Q25/39, 2022 Q9, sep2023 Q7
Esophageal hiatus (T10, right crus) transmits the oesophagus + vagus nerves2021B Q25/39
Caval opening (T8) transmits the IVC + right phrenic nerve2021B Q39
Below the arcuate line (of Douglas) ALL aponeuroses pass ANTERIOR to rectus abdominis; the posterior sheath is deficient (only transversalis fascia behind)2021B Q3/11, 2022 Q16, 2023 Q10
Posterior layer of the rectus sheath (above the arcuate line) = internal oblique (posterior lamina) + transversus abdominis aponeurosis2023 Q10
Linea semilunaris (of Spigelius) = the lateral curved border of the rectus abdominis (tendinous junction of the flat muscles)2021B Q31/48, sep2020 Q25
Cremaster muscle is derived from the internal oblique (with a contribution from transversus); passes through the inguinal canalsep2020 Q1/25, sep2023 Q7
Inguinal canal: external oblique aponeurosis = anterior wall; transversalis fascia = posterior wall; roof = arched internal oblique/transversus; floor = inguinal ligamentsep2020 Q1
Abdominal muscles: rotate & laterally flex the trunk, flex the trunk, and raise intra-abdominal pressure — they do NOT elevate the ribs2020 Q35
Serratus anterior = long thoracic nerve; protracts the scapula & holds it to the chest wall (winging if lost)2020 Q11, 2021A Q9/46
Oblique abdominal muscles are innervated by the lower intercostal (thoracoabdominal) nerves2021B Q31/48, sep2020 Q25
Quadratus lumborum attaches to the iliac crest & 12th rib; extends/laterally flexes the trunksep2020 Q29, sep2023 Q8

DIAPHRAGM OPENINGS — MEMORISE LEVEL + CONTENTS ("I ATE TEN EGGS AT TWELVE")

Opening (vertebral level)Transmits
Caval opening (T8) — in central tendonInferior vena cava + right phrenic nerve
Esophageal hiatus (T10) — right crusOesophagus + anterior & posterior vagal trunks
Aortic hiatus (T12) — behind the cruraDescending (thoracic) aorta, thoracic duct, azygos vein
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False — the aorta passes the aortic hiatus (T12).
False — only the RIGHT phrenic nerve accompanies the IVC; the vagi go with the oesophagus.
False — below it there is NO posterior aponeurotic sheath.
False — that is inspiration; abdominals aid expiration/compression.
False — internal oblique (+ transversus).

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • Learn the diaphragm openings as level + contents pairs — this is a near-guaranteed 1–2 marks and the trap is always swapping aorta/oesophagus.
  • Understand the arcuate line concept once (below it, everything goes in front of rectus) rather than memorising four layers blindly.
  • Keep inguinal-canal walls on a single card (anterior = EO, posterior = transversalis fascia, floor = inguinal lig., roof = arching IO/TA).
Mnemonics
Diaphragm levels: "I (IVC) 8, ten (10) eggs (esophagus), at 12 (aorta)" → T8 IVC, T10 oesophagus, T12 aorta.
Caval opening: IVC + Right phrenic = "I Ate a Right one" (both start on the right).
Aortic hiatus contents: "Aorta, thoracic Duct, Azygos" → the structures that go BEHIND the diaphragm.

sections.title: 12 · Muscles of the Upper Limb
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

This is a muscle–nerve–action chapter above all: the rotator cuff (insertions + nerves), the arm flexors/extensors (musculocutaneous vs radial), and the forearm compartments (anterior = mostly median/ulnar; posterior = radial). Clinical "which nerve is injured" vignettes (can't extend the wrist = radial; can't supinate = radial + musculocutaneous) recur.

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Rotator cuff insertions: Supraspinatus/Infraspinatus/Teres minor → GREATER tubercle; Subscapularis → LESSER tubercle2020 Q29, 2021A Q4/13/39, 2023 Q32, sep2020 Q18/34/37
Teres minor is innervated by the AXILLARY nerve; supra-/infraspinatus by the suprascapular nerve; subscapularis by the subscapular nerves2021A Q13, sep2020 Q42, sep2023 Q4
Deltoid: axillary nerve; abducts the arm (15–90°); inserts on the deltoid tuberosity2021B Q28, 2021A Q4
Biceps brachii: musculocutaneous nerve; flexes the elbow AND is a powerful SUPINATOR; long head = supraglenoid tubercle2020 Q13, 2022 Q1, sep2020 Q45
Brachialis (musculocutaneous) inserts on the ulnar tuberosity — the pure elbow flexor2021A Q15, sep2020 Q45
Coracobrachialis: origin coracoid process, musculocutaneous nerve; stabilises the humeral head2020s, 2021A Q9, 2021B Q37, sep2020 Q22
Triceps brachii (posterior arm) + anconeus = radial nerve; long head origin = infraglenoid tubercle2021A Q44, 2021B Q7/32/35
Median nerve supplies flexor carpi radialis, palmaris longus, pronator teres, FDS, FPL, pronator quadratus (via AIN); ulnar nerve supplies flexor carpi ulnaris + the interossei/adductor pollicis2020 Q12/42, 2021B Q22, 2023 Q12/25/37, sep2020 Q33/35
Radial nerve: brachioradialis, all wrist/finger extensors, supinator, anconeus. Injury → wrist drop (cannot extend wrist)2021B Q32/33/35, 2022 Q11, sep2020 Q12
Extensor carpi ulnaris = wrist extension + ulnar deviation; brachioradialis = elbow flexion (radial nerve)2020 Q1/17, 2022 Q13
Interossei ab/adduct the fingers (dorsal ABduct, palmar ADduct) — all ulnar nerve2022 Q10
Serratus anterior (long thoracic n.) is functionally an "upper limb" scapular muscle; pectoralis major adducts/medially rotates the arm2020 Q11/14

ROTATOR CUFF (SITS) — THE SINGLE MOST-TESTED MUSCLE GROUP IN THE PAPER

MuscleInsertion · Nerve · Action
SupraspinatusGreater tubercle (superior facet) · suprascapular n. · initiates abduction (0–15°)
InfraspinatusGreater tubercle (middle facet) · suprascapular n. · lateral rotation
Teres minorGreater tubercle (inferior facet) · axillary n. · lateral rotation
SubscapularisLESSER tubercle · upper & lower subscapular n. · medial rotation

FOREARM MUSCLE → NERVE (LEARN THE EXCEPTIONS)

NerveSupplies
Median (incl. AIN)Pronator teres, flexor carpi radialis, palmaris longus, FDS, lateral FDP, FPL, pronator quadratus, thenar (LOAF)
UlnarFlexor carpi ulnaris, medial FDP, all interossei, adductor pollicis, hypothenar, 3rd & 4th lumbricals
Radial (+ deep/PIN)Brachioradialis, ALL extensors, supinator, anconeus
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False — subscapularis → LESSER tubercle.
False — it ABducts; axillary nerve.
False — it is a powerful SUPINATOR.
False — ulnar nerve (one of only 1½ anterior-forearm muscles it supplies).
False — radial (deep branch / PIN).

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • Memorise SITS as a block (insertion + nerve + action) — it is worth several marks every paper.
  • Learn the two anterior-forearm exceptions to the median nerve: flexor carpi ulnaris + medial half of FDP = ULNAR. Everything else anterior = median.
  • Attach a clinical sign to each nerve: radial → wrist drop; median → "hand of benediction"/loss of thumb opposition; ulnar → claw hand/loss of interossei.
Mnemonics
Rotator cuff: "SITS" — Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor, Subscapularis (first three → greater tubercle, last → lesser).
Musculocutaneous muscles: "BBC" — Biceps, Brachialis, Coracobrachialis.
Median hand muscles: "LOAF" — Lumbricals 1&2, Opponens pollicis, Abductor pollicis brevis, Flexor pollicis brevis.

sections.title: 13 · Muscles of the Lower Limb (Thigh, Leg & Foot)
sections.html:
WHAT THE EXAM ACTUALLY TESTS HERE

Quadriceps vs hamstrings (heads, action, nerve); the leg compartments — anterior (deep fibular n., dorsiflexion), lateral (superficial fibular n., eversion), posterior (tibial n., plantarflexion); tibialis anterior detail; and the popliteal fossa boundaries. Muscle→nerve and muscle→action associations dominate.

HIGH-YIELD REPEATS (ALREADY TESTED — LEARN COLD)

High-yield factPapers / how it is tested
Quadriceps femoris = rectus femoris + vastus lateralis + vastus medialis + vastus intermedius; extends the knee; inserts via the patella/patellar ligament; femoral nerve2020 Q15, 2021A Q41, 2021B Q24/27, 2022 Q8, sep2020 Q31
Hamstrings (semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris) = sciatic nerve; flex the knee & extend the hip; origin ischial tuberosity (biceps short head from linea aspera)2020 Q39/47, 2021B Q45, sep2020 Q30, 2023 Q30
Biceps femoris LATERALLY rotates the flexed knee (semi-tendinosus/-membranosus rotate it medially)2021B Q45
Tibialis anterior: deep fibular nerve; dorsiflexes + inverts; origin = upper 2/3 tibia + interosseous membrane; inserts on the medial cuneiform + base of 1st metatarsal2021A Q8, 2022 Q2, 2023 Q16, sep2020 Q20
Dorsiflexion of the foot = tibialis anterior + extensor digitorum longus (both deep fibular n.)2021B Q38, sep2020 Q20/24
Fibularis (peroneus) longus & brevis = SUPERFICIAL fibular nerve; evert the foot2021B Q38, 2023 Q5
Posterior-leg muscles (gastrocnemius, soleus, popliteus, tibialis posterior, FHL, FDL) = tibial nerve2020 Q47, 2023 Q5/14/29, sep2020 Q6
Popliteal fossa borders: superolaterally biceps femoris, superomedially semimembranosus/semitendinosus, inferiorly the two heads of gastrocnemius (tibialis posterior is NOT a border)2020 Q47
Gracilis + adductors participate in adduction; sartorius (ASIS, femoral n.) flexes hip & knee2023 Q40, 2021B Q8
Adductor canal (of Hunter): bounded by vastus medialis, adductor longus/magnus, and the vastoadductor membrane; links the femoral triangle to the popliteal fossa2022 Q12
Hallux muscles: abductor hallucis, adductor hallucis, flexor hallucis brevis (intrinsic); flexor hallucis longus is a deep posterior-leg musclesep2020 Q44, sep2023 Q25

LEG COMPARTMENTS — COMPARTMENT ↔ NERVE ↔ ACTION

CompartmentNerve · Muscles · Action
AnteriorDeep fibular n. · tibialis anterior, EDL, EHL, fibularis tertius · DORSIFLEXION + inversion
LateralSuperficial fibular n. · fibularis longus & brevis · EVERSION + weak plantarflexion
PosteriorTibial n. · gastrocnemius, soleus, plantaris, popliteus, TP, FDL, FHL · PLANTARFLEXION + inversion
Traps the examiner reuses (the false option → the truth):
False — it EXTENDS the knee (only sartorius/hamstrings flex).
False — DEEP fibular nerve; it dorsiflexes.
False — superficial fibular nerve.
False — flexes hip & knee, femoral nerve.
False — the borders are biceps femoris, semi-M/T, and gastrocnemius heads.

HOW TO STUDY THIS CHAPTER

  • Learn the three leg compartments as compartment→nerve→action triads — almost every leg question is one of these three.
  • Fix "quadriceps EXTEND, hamstrings FLEX" and the biceps femoris lateral-rotation exception.
  • Note the two dorsiflexors (TA + EDL) and two evertors (fibularis longus + brevis) as fixed answer-pairs the exam reuses verbatim.
Mnemonics
Compartments: Anterior = "D-Fib Dorsi" (Deep Fibular, Dorsiflex); Lateral = "S-Fib Evert" (Superficial Fibular, Evert); Posterior = "Tibial Plantar."
Quads: "Really Very Very Vulnerable" → Rectus femoris, Vastus Lateralis/Medialis/Intermedius.
Foot drop: Deep fibular n. injury → cannot dorsiflex → foot drop (matches the anterior compartment).

Final revision loop

For the last week: read only the amber "high-yield repeats" tables and the red "traps" boxes. Those two together contain essentially every fact these seven papers have ever tested. Turn each repeat row into an Anki cloze, drill the traps as "spot-the-false-statement" cards, and you will be matching the exam's own pattern rather than studying blind.
